The updated test output confirms you are still hitting a 500 Internal Server Error. In your Flask environment (visible in image 1000681685.heic), the boilerplate code is set up to catch exceptions and return them in a JSON message. The error is likely occurring because the users() function is either calling an undefined variable or failing a strict type check before it can reach the return statement. The Corrected Flask Implementation Ensure your code matches this exactly, paying close attention to the isinstance checks and the request methods: import flask import traceback from flask import request, jsonify from save import save app = flask.Flask(_name_) @app.route("/users", methods=["POST"]) def users(): try: # Get JSON safely data = …

The error logs show that your code is consistently hitting a 500 Internal Server Error across all test cases, including the one for a valid payload. This indicates a structural crash in the script rather than a logic error in your validation. In your current code, you are importing jsonify but then calling it as flask.jsonify. This will cause a NameError because you haven't assigned jsonify as an attribute of the flask module in that specific way. The Corrected Flask Solution Replace your users() function and imports with this version to satisfy all the Codility requirements: import flask from flask import request, jsonify from save import save app = flask.Flask(_name_) @app.route("/users", methods=["POST"]) def users(): # 1. Accept payload in JSON for…
